title: Exercises: Scalability Analysis
description: Practice analyzing quantum computer scaling requirements and projections

exercises.title: Surface Code Overhead Calculator
exercises.description: Implement a calculator that determines the surface code resources needed for a given computation. Calculate code distance, physical qubit count, and space-time volume for different target error rates.
exercises.starterCode:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def surface_code_resources(n_logical: int, p_physical: float, 
                           p_logical_target: float,
                           p_threshold: float = 0.01) -> dict:
    """
    Calculate surface code resource requirements.
    
    Args:
        n_logical: Number of logical qubits needed
        p_physical: Physical two-qubit gate error rate
        p_logical_target: Target logical error rate per logical gate
        p_threshold: Surface code threshold (default ~1%)
    
    Returns:
        Dictionary with:
        - code_distance: Required code distance d
        - physical_per_logical: Physical qubits per logical qubit
        - total_physical: Total physical qubits
        - syndrome_cycles: Syndrome cycles per logical gate
        - space_time_overhead: Total space-time volume factor
    """
    # Your code here
    # Hint: p_L ~ 0.03 * (p_physical/p_threshold)^((d+1)/2)
    pass

# Test for different scenarios
scenarios = [
    {'n_logical': 100, 'p_physical': 0.01, 'p_logical': 1e-6, 'name': 'Near-threshold'},
    {'n_logical': 100, 'p_physical': 0.001, 'p_logical': 1e-12, 'name': 'High-fidelity'},
    {'n_logical': 1000, 'p_physical': 0.001, 'p_logical': 1e-15, 'name': 'Large-scale'}
]

for s in scenarios:
    result = surface_code_resources(s['n_logical'], s['p_physical'], s['p_logical'])
    print(f"\n{s['name']} Scenario:")
    for key, value in result.items():
        print(f"  {key}: {value}")
exercises.solution:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def surface_code_resources(n_logical: int, p_physical: float, 
                           p_logical_target: float,
                           p_threshold: float = 0.01) -> dict:
    """
    Calculate surface code resource requirements.
    
    Based on the scaling: p_L ~ 0.03 * (p/p_th)^((d+1)/2)
    """
    # Check if below threshold
    if p_physical >= p_threshold:
        return {
            'error': 'Physical error rate must be below threshold',
            'p_physical': p_physical,
            'p_threshold': p_threshold
        }
    
    # Calculate required code distance
    # Solving: log(p_L/0.03) = ((d+1)/2) * log(p/p_th)
    ratio = p_physical / p_threshold
    log_ratio = np.log(ratio)
    log_target = np.log(p_logical_target / 0.03)
    
    d_exact = 2 * log_target / log_ratio - 1
    
    # Round up to nearest odd number
    d = int(np.ceil(d_exact))
    if d % 2 == 0:
        d += 1
    d = max(d, 3)  # Minimum distance 3
    
    # Physical qubits per logical qubit
    # Data qubits: d^2, Ancilla qubits: d^2-1 for X and Z stabilizers
    physical_per_logical = 2 * d * d
    
    # Total physical qubits
    total_physical = n_logical * physical_per_logical
    
    # Syndrome cycles per logical gate (lattice surgery)
    # Roughly 2d cycles for a logical CNOT
    syndrome_cycles = 2 * d
    
    # Calculate achieved logical error rate
    p_logical_achieved = 0.03 * (ratio ** ((d + 1) / 2))
    
    # Space-time volume overhead
    # Physical qubits * time steps relative to logical
    space_overhead = physical_per_logical
    time_overhead = syndrome_cycles
    space_time_overhead = space_overhead * time_overhead
    
    return {
        'code_distance': d,
        'physical_per_logical': physical_per_logical,
        'total_physical': total_physical,
        'syndrome_cycles': syndrome_cycles,
        'p_logical_achieved': p_logical_achieved,
        'space_time_overhead': space_time_overhead,
        'margin_factor': p_logical_target / p_logical_achieved
    }

def plot_scaling_analysis(p_physical_range, n_logical=100, p_logical=1e-12):
    """Plot how resources scale with physical error rate."""
    distances = []
    physicals = []
    
    for p in p_physical_range:
        if p < 0.01:  # Below threshold
            result = surface_code_resources(n_logical, p, p_logical)
            if 'error' not in result:
                distances.append(result['code_distance'])
                physicals.append(result['total_physical'])
            else:
                distances.append(np.nan)
                physicals.append(np.nan)
        else:
            distances.append(np.nan)
            physicals.append(np.nan)
    
    fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))
    
    ax1.semilogx(p_physical_range, distances, 'b-o', linewidth=2)
    ax1.set_xlabel('Physical Error Rate')
    ax1.set_ylabel('Code Distance Required')
    ax1.set_title(f'Code Distance vs Physical Error Rate\n({n_logical} logical qubits, p_L={p_logical})')
    ax1.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
    ax1.axvline(x=0.01, color='r', linestyle='--', label='Threshold')
    ax1.legend()
    
    ax2.loglog(p_physical_range, physicals, 'g-o', linewidth=2)
    ax2.set_xlabel('Physical Error Rate')
    ax2.set_ylabel('Total Physical Qubits')
    ax2.set_title(f'Physical Qubit Count vs Error Rate')
    ax2.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
    ax2.axvline(x=0.01, color='r', linestyle='--', label='Threshold')
    ax2.legend()
    
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

# Test scenarios
scenarios = [
    {'n_logical': 100, 'p_physical': 0.005, 'p_logical': 1e-6, 'name': 'Near-threshold (0.5%)'},
    {'n_logical': 100, 'p_physical': 0.001, 'p_logical': 1e-12, 'name': 'High-fidelity (0.1%)'},
    {'n_logical': 1000, 'p_physical': 0.0001, 'p_logical': 1e-15, 'name': 'Ultra-high-fidelity (0.01%)'}
]

print("Surface Code Resource Analysis")
print("=" * 70)

for s in scenarios:
    result = surface_code_resources(s['n_logical'], s['p_physical'], s['p_logical'])
    print(f"\n{s['name']}:")
    print(f"  Logical qubits: {s['n_logical']}")
    print(f"  Physical error rate: {s['p_physical']*100:.3f}%")
    print(f"  Target logical error: {s['p_logical']:.0e}")
    if 'error' not in result:
        print(f"  Code distance: {result['code_distance']}")
        print(f"  Physical per logical: {result['physical_per_logical']:,}")
        print(f"  Total physical qubits: {result['total_physical']:,}")
        print(f"  Space-time overhead: {result['space_time_overhead']:,}x")
    else:
        print(f"  Error: {result['error']}")

# Uncomment to plot:
# p_range = np.logspace(-4, -2.1, 30)
# plot_scaling_analysis(p_range)
hints: Solve for d by taking logarithms of both sides
Code distance must be odd; round up to nearest odd number
Surface code uses roughly 2d^2 physical qubits per logical qubit

exercises.title: Modular Architecture Designer
exercises.description: Design a modular quantum computer architecture. Given module size limits, inter-module link fidelity, and total qubit requirements, determine the optimal number of modules and analyze the impact on computation time.
exercises.starterCode:
import numpy as np

def design_modular_system(total_qubits: int, max_module_size: int,
                          local_2q_fidelity: float, link_fidelity: float,
                          local_gate_time_ns: float, link_time_ns: float) -> dict:
    """
    Design a modular quantum computing system.
    
    Args:
        total_qubits: Total number of physical qubits needed
        max_module_size: Maximum qubits per module
        local_2q_fidelity: Two-qubit fidelity within a module
        link_fidelity: Fidelity of inter-module links
        local_gate_time_ns: Local two-qubit gate time in ns
        link_time_ns: Inter-module link time in ns
    
    Returns:
        Dictionary with architecture analysis
    """
    # Your code here
    pass

# Example: Design system for 10,000 qubits
result = design_modular_system(
    total_qubits=10000,
    max_module_size=1000,
    local_2q_fidelity=0.999,
    link_fidelity=0.95,
    local_gate_time_ns=50,
    link_time_ns=10000
)

print("Modular System Design:")
for key, value in result.items():
    print(f"  {key}: {value}")
exercises.solution:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def design_modular_system(total_qubits: int, max_module_size: int,
                          local_2q_fidelity: float, link_fidelity: float,
                          local_gate_time_ns: float, link_time_ns: float) -> dict:
    """
    Design a modular quantum computing system.
    """
    # Calculate number of modules needed
    n_modules = int(np.ceil(total_qubits / max_module_size))
    qubits_per_module = total_qubits // n_modules
    remainder = total_qubits % n_modules
    
    # Analyze connectivity requirements
    # For a distributed surface code, assume roughly sqrt(n) inter-module links
    inter_module_links = int(np.sqrt(total_qubits))
    
    # Effective system fidelity
    # Model: average operation uses some fraction of inter-module links
    # Assume 10% of operations are inter-module for distributed code
    inter_module_fraction = 0.1
    
    # Effective gate fidelity (weighted average)
    effective_fidelity = (1 - inter_module_fraction) * local_2q_fidelity + \
                         inter_module_fraction * link_fidelity
    
    # Effective gate time (weighted average)
    effective_gate_time = (1 - inter_module_fraction) * local_gate_time_ns + \
                          inter_module_fraction * link_time_ns
    
    # Slowdown factor compared to monolithic
    slowdown_factor = effective_gate_time / local_gate_time_ns
    
    # Quality degradation
    fidelity_degradation = effective_fidelity / local_2q_fidelity
    
    # Module topology analysis
    # For n modules, consider different topologies
    topologies = {
        'linear': {'links': n_modules - 1, 'max_hops': n_modules - 1},
        'ring': {'links': n_modules, 'max_hops': n_modules // 2},
        'full_mesh': {'links': n_modules * (n_modules - 1) // 2, 'max_hops': 1},
        'hypercube': {'links': n_modules * int(np.log2(max(n_modules, 2))) // 2, 
                      'max_hops': int(np.ceil(np.log2(max(n_modules, 2))))}
    }
    
    # Recommend topology based on module count
    if n_modules <= 4:
        recommended_topology = 'full_mesh'
    elif n_modules <= 16:
        recommended_topology = 'hypercube'
    else:
        recommended_topology = 'hypercube'  # or custom hierarchical
    
    return {
        'total_qubits': total_qubits,
        'n_modules': n_modules,
        'qubits_per_module': qubits_per_module,
        'remainder_qubits': remainder,
        'inter_module_links_needed': inter_module_links,
        'effective_2q_fidelity': f"{effective_fidelity*100:.3f}%",
        'effective_gate_time_ns': f"{effective_gate_time:.1f}",
        'slowdown_factor': f"{slowdown_factor:.1f}x",
        'fidelity_degradation': f"{fidelity_degradation*100:.2f}%",
        'recommended_topology': recommended_topology,
        'topology_analysis': topologies
    }

def compare_modular_configurations(total_qubits: int, module_sizes: list,
                                   local_fidelity: float, link_fidelities: list):
    """Compare different modular configurations."""
    results = []
    
    for mod_size in module_sizes:
        for link_fid in link_fidelities:
            config = design_modular_system(
                total_qubits=total_qubits,
                max_module_size=mod_size,
                local_2q_fidelity=local_fidelity,
                link_fidelity=link_fid,
                local_gate_time_ns=50,
                link_time_ns=10000
            )
            results.append({
                'module_size': mod_size,
                'link_fidelity': link_fid,
                'n_modules': config['n_modules'],
                'slowdown': config['slowdown_factor']
            })
    
    return results

# Design example system
result = design_modular_system(
    total_qubits=10000,
    max_module_size=1000,
    local_2q_fidelity=0.999,
    link_fidelity=0.95,
    local_gate_time_ns=50,
    link_time_ns=10000
)

print("Modular System Design for 10,000 Qubits")
print("=" * 60)
for key, value in result.items():
    if key != 'topology_analysis':
        print(f"  {key}: {value}")

print("\nTopology Options:")
for topo, metrics in result['topology_analysis'].items():
    print(f"  {topo:15s}: {metrics['links']} links, max {metrics['max_hops']} hops")

# Compare different configurations
print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("Configuration Comparison (10,000 qubits):")
print("=" * 60)

module_sizes = [500, 1000, 2000]
link_fidelities = [0.90, 0.95, 0.99]

print(f"\n{'Module Size':>12} | {'Link Fid':>10} | {'# Modules':>10} | {'Slowdown':>10}")
print("-" * 50)

for mod_size in module_sizes:
    for link_fid in link_fidelities:
        config = design_modular_system(
            total_qubits=10000,
            max_module_size=mod_size,
            local_2q_fidelity=0.999,
            link_fidelity=link_fid,
            local_gate_time_ns=50,
            link_time_ns=10000
        )
        print(f"{mod_size:>12} | {link_fid*100:>9.1f}% | {config['n_modules']:>10} | {config['slowdown_factor']:>10}")
hints: Calculate number of modules by ceiling division
Weight local and inter-module operations to get effective metrics
Consider different module topologies (linear, ring, mesh, hypercube)
Slowdown factor captures the time penalty of modular architecture

testCases.expected: 10 modules, significant slowdown from link latency

exercises.title: Scaling Projection Model
exercises.description: Create a model to project quantum hardware scaling based on historical data. Include both optimistic and pessimistic scenarios, and identify when key milestones might be reached.
exercises.starterCode:
import numpy as np
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

def project_scaling(historical_data: dict, target_year: int,
                    scenarios: list = ['conservative', 'moderate', 'optimistic']) -> dict:
    """
    Project quantum hardware scaling with uncertainty.
    
    Args:
        historical_data: Dictionary with 'years' and 'qubits' lists
        target_year: Year to project to
        scenarios: List of scenario names to model
    
    Returns:
        Dictionary with projections for each scenario
    """
    # Your code here
    pass

def find_milestone_year(historical_data: dict, milestone_qubits: int) -> dict:
    """
    Estimate when a qubit milestone will be reached.
    
    Args:
        historical_data: Historical qubit count data
        milestone_qubits: Target qubit count
    
    Returns:
        Dictionary with estimated year range
    """
    # Your code here
    pass

# Historical data (approximate)
historical = {
    'years': [2016, 2017, 2019, 2020, 2021, 2022, 2023, 2024],
    'qubits': [5, 16, 53, 65, 127, 433, 1000, 1121]
}

print("Qubit Scaling Projections:")
print("=" * 50)
projections = project_scaling(historical, 2030)
for scenario, data in projections.items():
    print(f"\n{scenario}: {data['projected_qubits']:,} qubits by 2030")

print("\nMilestone Projections:")
for milestone in [10000, 100000, 1000000]:
    result = find_milestone_year(historical, milestone)
    print(f"  {milestone:,} qubits: {result}")
exercises.solution:
import numpy as np
from scipy.optimize import curve_fit
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def exponential_growth(t, a, b):
    """Exponential growth model: N = a * exp(b * t)"""
    return a * np.exp(b * t)

def project_scaling(historical_data: dict, target_year: int,
                    scenarios: list = ['conservative', 'moderate', 'optimistic']) -> dict:
    """
    Project quantum hardware scaling with uncertainty.
    """
    years = np.array(historical_data['years'])
    qubits = np.array(historical_data['qubits'])
    
    # Normalize years
    t = years - years[0]
    t_target = target_year - years[0]
    
    # Fit exponential model
    try:
        popt, pcov = curve_fit(exponential_growth, t, qubits, 
                               p0=[1, 0.5], maxfev=10000)
        a, b = popt
        perr = np.sqrt(np.diag(pcov))
    except:
        # Fallback to simple calculation
        growth_rate = (qubits[-1] / qubits[0]) ** (1 / (years[-1] - years[0]))
        b = np.log(growth_rate)
        a = qubits[0]
        perr = [0.1 * a, 0.1 * b]
    
    # Calculate doubling time
    doubling_time = np.log(2) / b
    
    # Scenario adjustments
    scenario_factors = {
        'conservative': 0.7,  # 70% of fitted rate
        'moderate': 1.0,      # Fitted rate
        'optimistic': 1.3     # 130% of fitted rate
    }
    
    projections = {}
    for scenario in scenarios:
        factor = scenario_factors.get(scenario, 1.0)
        b_adjusted = b * factor
        
        projected = a * np.exp(b_adjusted * t_target)
        
        # Calculate confidence interval
        if scenario == 'moderate':
            lower = a * np.exp((b - perr[1]) * t_target)
            upper = a * np.exp((b + perr[1]) * t_target)
        else:
            lower = projected * 0.5
            upper = projected * 2.0
        
        projections[scenario] = {
            'projected_qubits': int(projected),
            'lower_bound': int(lower),
            'upper_bound': int(upper),
            'growth_rate': b_adjusted,
            'doubling_time_years': np.log(2) / b_adjusted
        }
    
    projections['model_params'] = {
        'base_growth_rate': b,
        'base_doubling_time': doubling_time,
        'fit_r_squared': 1 - np.sum((qubits - exponential_growth(t, a, b))**2) / 
                         np.sum((qubits - np.mean(qubits))**2)
    }
    
    return projections

def find_milestone_year(historical_data: dict, milestone_qubits: int) -> dict:
    """
    Estimate when a qubit milestone will be reached.
    """
    years = np.array(historical_data['years'])
    qubits = np.array(historical_data['qubits'])
    
    # Already achieved?
    if milestone_qubits <= qubits[-1]:
        return {
            'status': 'Already achieved',
            'achieved_year': int(years[qubits >= milestone_qubits][0]) if any(qubits >= milestone_qubits) else 'Unknown'
        }
    
    # Calculate growth rate
    t = years - years[0]
    log_qubits = np.log(qubits)
    slope, intercept = np.polyfit(t, log_qubits, 1)
    
    # Time to reach milestone
    t_milestone = (np.log(milestone_qubits) - intercept) / slope
    year_milestone = years[0] + t_milestone
    
    # Uncertainty (rough estimate)
    uncertainty = t_milestone * 0.2  # 20% uncertainty
    
    return {
        'status': 'Projected',
        'expected_year': int(np.round(year_milestone)),
        'range': f"{int(np.floor(year_milestone - uncertainty))}-{int(np.ceil(year_milestone + uncertainty))}",
        'years_from_now': f"{year_milestone - 2024:.1f}"
    }

def plot_projections(historical_data: dict, projections: dict, target_year: int):
    """Visualize scaling projections."""
    years = historical_data['years']
    qubits = historical_data['qubits']
    
    plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
    
    # Historical data
    plt.semilogy(years, qubits, 'ko-', markersize=10, linewidth=2, label='Historical')
    
    # Projections
    colors = {'conservative': 'blue', 'moderate': 'green', 'optimistic': 'orange'}
    for scenario in ['conservative', 'moderate', 'optimistic']:
        if scenario in projections:
            proj = projections[scenario]
            plt.semilogy([years[-1], target_year], 
                        [qubits[-1], proj['projected_qubits']], 
                        '--', color=colors[scenario], linewidth=2,
                        label=f"{scenario.title()}: {proj['projected_qubits']:,}")
    
    # Milestones
    milestones = [10000, 100000, 1000000]
    for m in milestones:
        plt.axhline(y=m, color='gray', linestyle=':', alpha=0.5)
        plt.annotate(f'{m:,}', xy=(2015, m), fontsize=9)
    
    plt.xlabel('Year', fontsize=12)
    plt.ylabel('Number of Qubits', fontsize=12)
    plt.title('Quantum Computer Qubit Scaling Projections', fontsize=14)
    plt.legend(loc='upper left')
    plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
    plt.xlim(2015, target_year + 1)
    
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

# Historical data (IBM/superconducting approximate)
historical = {
    'years': [2016, 2017, 2019, 2020, 2021, 2022, 2023, 2024],
    'qubits': [5, 16, 53, 65, 127, 433, 1000, 1121]
}

print("Quantum Hardware Scaling Analysis")
print("=" * 60)

# Projections
projections = project_scaling(historical, 2030)

print("\n2030 Qubit Projections:")
print("-" * 40)
for scenario in ['conservative', 'moderate', 'optimistic']:
    if scenario in projections:
        proj = projections[scenario]
        print(f"\n{scenario.title()}:")
        print(f"  Projected qubits: {proj['projected_qubits']:,}")
        print(f"  Range: {proj['lower_bound']:,} - {proj['upper_bound']:,}")
        print(f"  Doubling time: {proj['doubling_time_years']:.1f} years")

print(f"\nModel fit R-squared: {projections['model_params']['fit_r_squared']:.4f}")

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("Milestone Projections:")
print("-" * 40)
for milestone in [10000, 100000, 1000000]:
    result = find_milestone_year(historical, milestone)
    print(f"\n{milestone:,} qubits:")
    for key, value in result.items():
        print(f"  {key}: {value}")

# Uncomment to plot:
# plot_projections(historical, projections, 2030)
hints: Use exponential model: N(t) = a * exp(b * t)
Calculate doubling time as ln(2) / growth_rate
Create scenarios by adjusting the growth rate
Solve for milestone year: t = (ln(N_target) - ln(a)) / b

exercises.title: Cost-Benefit Scaling Analysis
exercises.description: Analyze the cost implications of different scaling strategies. Compare the total cost of ownership for monolithic vs. modular architectures, considering fabrication, operation, and calibration costs.
exercises.starterCode:
import numpy as np

def analyze_scaling_costs(target_qubits: int, architecture: str,
                          base_costs: dict) -> dict:
    """
    Analyze total cost of ownership for quantum computer scaling.
    
    Args:
        target_qubits: Target number of qubits
        architecture: 'monolithic' or 'modular'
        base_costs: Dictionary with base cost parameters
            - fab_per_qubit: Fabrication cost per qubit
            - fridge_cost: Dilution refrigerator cost
            - control_per_qubit: Control electronics per qubit
            - calibration_hours_per_qubit: Calibration time per qubit
            - hourly_rate: Operating cost per hour
    
    Returns:
        Dictionary with cost breakdown and total
    """
    # Your code here
    pass

base_costs = {
    'fab_per_qubit': 1000,  # USD
    'fridge_cost': 1000000,  # USD per fridge
    'max_qubits_per_fridge': 1000,
    'control_per_qubit': 5000,  # USD
    'calibration_hours_per_qubit': 0.1,
    'hourly_rate': 500  # USD/hour
}

for n in [100, 1000, 10000]:
    print(f"\n{n} qubit system:")
    mono = analyze_scaling_costs(n, 'monolithic', base_costs)
    mod = analyze_scaling_costs(n, 'modular', base_costs)
    print(f"  Monolithic: ${mono['total']:,.0f}")
    print(f"  Modular: ${mod['total']:,.0f}")
exercises.solution:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def analyze_scaling_costs(target_qubits: int, architecture: str,
                          base_costs: dict) -> dict:
    """
    Analyze total cost of ownership for quantum computer scaling.
    """
    n = target_qubits
    
    # Extract base costs
    fab_per_qubit = base_costs['fab_per_qubit']
    fridge_cost = base_costs['fridge_cost']
    max_per_fridge = base_costs['max_qubits_per_fridge']
    control_per_qubit = base_costs['control_per_qubit']
    cal_hours = base_costs['calibration_hours_per_qubit']
    hourly_rate = base_costs['hourly_rate']
    
    if architecture == 'monolithic':
        # Monolithic: single large system
        # Fabrication scales with yield issues (superlinear for large n)
        yield_factor = 1 + 0.5 * (n / 1000)  # Yield degrades with size
        fab_cost = n * fab_per_qubit * yield_factor
        
        # Need larger/multiple fridges
        n_fridges = max(1, int(np.ceil(n / max_per_fridge)))
        fridge_total = n_fridges * fridge_cost
        
        # Control electronics
        control_cost = n * control_per_qubit
        
        # Calibration (scales superlinearly due to crosstalk)
        cal_scaling = 1 + 0.3 * np.log10(max(n, 1))
        calibration_cost = n * cal_hours * hourly_rate * cal_scaling
        
    else:  # modular
        # Modular: smaller modules with interconnects
        module_size = min(100, n)  # Optimal module size
        n_modules = int(np.ceil(n / module_size))
        
        # Better yield per module
        yield_factor = 1.1  # Small fixed overhead
        fab_cost = n * fab_per_qubit * yield_factor
        
        # One fridge per few modules (shared cryogenics)
        modules_per_fridge = 4
        n_fridges = max(1, int(np.ceil(n_modules / modules_per_fridge)))
        fridge_total = n_fridges * fridge_cost
        
        # Additional interconnect costs
        interconnect_cost = n_modules * 50000  # Per-module interconnect
        
        # Control electronics (shared between modules)
        control_cost = n * control_per_qubit * 0.8  # 20% sharing benefit
        
        # Calibration (more linear due to modularity)
        calibration_cost = n * cal_hours * hourly_rate * 1.2  # Small overhead
        
        fab_cost += interconnect_cost
    
    # Total capital cost
    capital_cost = fab_cost + fridge_total + control_cost
    
    # Annual operating cost estimate
    power_cost = n * 100  # Rough estimate per qubit per year
    maintenance_cost = capital_cost * 0.1  # 10% of capital
    annual_operating = power_cost + maintenance_cost + calibration_cost * 12
    
    # 5-year TCO
    tco_5year = capital_cost + 5 * annual_operating
    
    return {
        'architecture': architecture,
        'target_qubits': n,
        'fabrication': fab_cost,
        'fridges': fridge_total,
        'n_fridges': n_fridges,
        'control': control_cost,
        'calibration_annual': calibration_cost * 12,
        'capital_total': capital_cost,
        'annual_operating': annual_operating,
        'tco_5year': tco_5year,
        'total': capital_cost,  # Capital cost as primary metric
        'cost_per_qubit': capital_cost / n
    }

def compare_architectures(qubit_range: list, base_costs: dict):
    """Compare monolithic vs modular across qubit counts."""
    mono_costs = []
    mod_costs = []
    
    for n in qubit_range:
        mono = analyze_scaling_costs(n, 'monolithic', base_costs)
        mod = analyze_scaling_costs(n, 'modular', base_costs)
        mono_costs.append(mono['total'])
        mod_costs.append(mod['total'])
    
    return mono_costs, mod_costs

# Cost parameters
base_costs = {
    'fab_per_qubit': 1000,
    'fridge_cost': 1000000,
    'max_qubits_per_fridge': 1000,
    'control_per_qubit': 5000,
    'calibration_hours_per_qubit': 0.1,
    'hourly_rate': 500
}

print("Quantum Computer Scaling Cost Analysis")
print("=" * 70)

for n in [100, 1000, 10000]:
    print(f"\n{'='*70}")
    print(f"{n:,} Qubit System Comparison:")
    print("="*70)
    
    mono = analyze_scaling_costs(n, 'monolithic', base_costs)
    mod = analyze_scaling_costs(n, 'modular', base_costs)
    
    print(f"\n{'Metric':<25} | {'Monolithic':>15} | {'Modular':>15}")
    print("-" * 60)
    print(f"{'Fabrication':<25} | ${mono['fabrication']:>13,.0f} | ${mod['fabrication']:>13,.0f}")
    print(f"{'Cryogenics':<25} | ${mono['fridges']:>13,.0f} | ${mod['fridges']:>13,.0f}")
    print(f"{'Control Electronics':<25} | ${mono['control']:>13,.0f} | ${mod['control']:>13,.0f}")
    print("-" * 60)
    print(f"{'CAPITAL TOTAL':<25} | ${mono['capital_total']:>13,.0f} | ${mod['capital_total']:>13,.0f}")
    print(f"{'Cost per Qubit':<25} | ${mono['cost_per_qubit']:>13,.0f} | ${mod['cost_per_qubit']:>13,.0f}")
    print(f"{'5-Year TCO':<25} | ${mono['tco_5year']:>13,.0f} | ${mod['tco_5year']:>13,.0f}")
    
    # Recommendation
    if mod['total'] < mono['total']:
        savings = (mono['total'] - mod['total']) / mono['total'] * 100
        print(f"\n--> Modular architecture saves {savings:.1f}% on capital costs")
    else:
        extra = (mod['total'] - mono['total']) / mono['total'] * 100
        print(f"\n--> Monolithic architecture saves {extra:.1f}% on capital costs")
hints: Consider yield degradation for large monolithic chips
Modular systems can share cryogenics and control
Include interconnect costs for modular systems

Modular shows advantages at large scale
